page.label: Everything you need to know
page.title: FAQ 🍕
page.description: Find answers to the most common questions about Cloud Native Days Italy 2026, and everything you need to plan your visit to Bologna.

faq.label: Frequently Asked Questions
faq.eyebrow: Did you try turning it off and on again?

items.question: How much does a ticket cost?
items.answer: The regular ticket is priced at 160€ (ex. VAT at 22%) and covers the full 2-day event. [Get your ticket](https://ti.to/apropos/cloud-native-days-italy-2026) on our ticketing page.

items.question: What does a ticket include?
items.answer: Your ticket includes 2-day access to all conference sessions, lunches on both days, workshops (subject to seat availability), and access to both the Sponsor Area and the Community/Open Source Area.

items.question: Can I attend only one day?
items.answer:
The ticket covers the full two-day event. We do not offer single-day passes or discounts for partial attendance.

The program is designed as a connected experience across both days. We would love to have you with us for all of it!

[Get your ticket](https://ti.to/apropos/cloud-native-days-italy-2026) and make the most of it!

items.question: How is the event structured?
items.answer: Cloud Native Days Italy 2026 takes place over two full days (May 18 and 19) at Savoia Hotel Regency in Bologna. The program runs across two parallel tracks and features keynotes, technical talks, lightning talks, and workshops. The venue also hosts a Sponsor Area and a Community/Open Source Area. Day 1 ends with an aperitivo for networking. [Check the agenda](/agenda) for the full program.

items.question: How many sessions are there?
items.answer: The program features 40+ sessions overall, spread across keynotes, technical talks, lightning talks, and workshops across two tracks and two days. [Check the agenda](/agenda) for the full schedule.

items.question: How do workshops work? Can I book a seat in advance?
items.answer:
Workshops are deep-dive, highly practical technical sessions: bring your own laptop to follow along hands-on.

Workshops are included in all tickets at no extra charge.

Seats are not bookable in advance: access is first-come, first-served, with a capacity of approximately 20 seats per workshop. There are four workshops in total: two in the morning and two in the afternoon, each lasting 4 hours.

We are really proud of the quality of these workshops, led by top-tier professionals. And we are proud to include them in all tickets (unlike many other events where sessions like these would cost a significant amount on their own).

Do not miss out. [Check the agenda](/agenda) for the full workshop schedule.

items.question: Will the talks be recorded?
items.answer: Yes. Talks from previous editions are available on our [YouTube channel](https://www.youtube.com/@CloudNativeDaysItaly/). We plan to publish recordings from CNDI 2026 as well; join our [Telegram community](https://t.me/cloudnativedaysitaly) or follow our socials to stay updated.

items.question: Is the event suitable for all experience levels?
items.answer: Absolutely. The program spans a wide range of topics and difficulty levels, from introductory sessions to advanced deep dives, covering cloud native, open source, and platform engineering. There is something for everyone, regardless of background or experience.

items.question: Will I receive a certificate of attendance?
items.answer: Yes. All attendees will receive a certificate of participation at the end of the event.

items.question: Where is the venue?
items.answer: The event is held at Savoia Hotel Regency, Via del Pilastro 2, Bologna, Italy. See the 'How to get here' section below for full directions.

items.question: I have a question not answered here. Who can I contact?
items.answer: Reach us at [org@cloudnativedaysitaly.org](mailto:org@cloudnativedaysitaly.org) and we will be happy to help.

routes.label: From Rome
steps: Take the A1 motorway heading toward Milan.
Take the A14 motorway exit toward Ancona / Bologna / Padova / Bologna ring road / Bologna Centro / Casalecchio.
Follow signs for the Bologna ring road (tangenziale), exit for S. Lazzaro di Savena / Ancona / Padova / Bologna / Centro / Airport / Casalecchio.
Take ring road exit 9 toward Bologna Centro / S. Donato.
Take the exit for Minerbio / Granarolo dell'Emilia / CAAB. Turn left onto Via S. Donato, then right onto Via del Pilastro — the Savoia Hotel Regency is there.

routes.label: From Milan
steps: Take the A1 motorway heading toward Rome.
Take the exit toward Bologna / Borgo Panigale / Ring road / Bologna Centro / Bologna Airport.
Follow signs for the ring road / Airport exit.
Take ring road exit 9 toward Bologna Centro / S. Donato.
Take the exit for Minerbio / Granarolo dell'Emilia / CAAB. Turn left onto Via S. Donato, then right onto Via del Pilastro — the Savoia Hotel Regency is there.

sections.title: By Plane
paragraphs: Bologna's Guglielmo Marconi Airport is approximately 10 km from the Savoia Hotel Regency.
The easiest options are taxi or Uber directly from the airport to the hotel.
Alternatively, take the Marconi Express to Bologna Central Station, then continue by public transport or taxi (approx. 6 km from the station to the venue).

sections.title: By Train
paragraphs: Bologna Central Station is served by high-speed and regional trains. Check timetables at Italo and Trenitalia.
From Bologna Central Station, the Savoia Hotel Regency is approximately 6 km away — reachable by public bus, taxi, or Uber.

sections.title: By Bus
paragraphs: Bologna's coach station is connected to major Italian and international destinations via ITABUS and FLIXBUS.
The coach station is just a few hundred meters from Bologna Central Station, from where you can reach the hotel by public transport, taxi, or Uber.

sections.title: Urban Buses & Taxi
sections.intro: Urban buses are operated by TPER.

buses.description: From Bologna Central Station toward Granarolo dell'Emilia.

usefulInfo.label: Useful info
usefulInfo.eyebrow: Enjoy your stay, and try not to break anything

cards.title: Weather in May
paragraphs: May is one of the best months to visit Bologna, with a mild and pleasant climate, ideal for exploring the historic center on foot. Highs frequently exceed **20-22 °C**, perfect for dining outdoors, while nighttime lows rarely drop below **10-12 °C**, making the days warm but not muggy, with cool evenings.
We recommend dressing in layers and bringing an umbrella, as spring showers are possible.

cards.title: What to visit
paragraphs: The heart of the city is **Piazza Maggiore**, facing the **Basilica of San Petronio**, dedicated to the city's patron saint. In addition to the central nave, its grand interior includes six square-plan bays with matching side bays, onto which 22 chapels (rich in works of art) open. Inside the Basilica is also the largest meridian line in the world: Cassini's meridian, completed in December 1657 and 66.8 meters long.

Also on the square stand the Palazzo del Podestà (13th century) with its Renaissance façade, and next to it the Fountain of Neptune designed by Giambologna (1563-66), the Palazzo dei Banchi, and the Palazzo d'Accursio, seat of the City Hall.

Also worth seeing are the **famous towers of Bologna**, currently closed to visitors due to restoration works: the Torre degli Asinelli (1109-19), commissioned by the Ghibelline nobleman Gherardo Asinelli, stands 97.20 meters tall and has withstood earthquakes and fires over the centuries. Built in the same period by other Ghibelline nobles, Filippo and Oddo Garisendi, the adjacent Torre della Garisenda is mentioned by Dante in the Divine Comedy in Canto XXXI of the Inferno (on the leaning side, a plaque recalls the poet's verses). Today it stands 48.16 meters tall, as it was lowered in the mid-14th century due to ground subsidence and has been known ever since as the 'stunted tower.'

Finally, one must mention the **porticoes** and the **Sanctuary of San Luca**, which can be reached via the longest portico in the world, designed by Francesco Dotti: 3.7 kilometers long, with 666 arches and 15 chapels. Since 1433, every year the Byzantine icon of the Madonna with Child, preserved on the main altar and attributed to Luke the Evangelist, is carried in procession along the portico from the Sanctuary to San Petronio.

cards.title: What to eat
paragraphs: Bologna is known as 'la grassa' ('the fat one'), and it's easy to understand why once you get a taste of its **rich and varied cuisine**, a cultural heritage passed down for generations.

The foundation of Bolognese cooking is **fresh egg pasta**, which forms the basis for **tagliatelle al ragù**, **tortellini in brodo** (strictly handmade, filled with mortadella, prosciutto crudo, and Parmigiano Reggiano), and **lasagne verdi alla bolognese**, made with green spinach pasta, ragù, and béchamel.

But Bolognese cuisine goes beyond pasta: **Mortadella di Bologna IGP** is a cured meat unique in the world, **Parmigiano Reggiano** is the king of cheeses, and boards of cured meats and cheeses served with **tigelle** filled with squacquerone, or with gnocco fritto, are not to be missed, best enjoyed alongside local wines.
